brief:
TOPIC: causes of early mortality after AF catheter ablation.

KNOWLEDGE GAP / WHY IT MATTERS:
- Patient safety after AF ablation depends on understanding what drives early death; causes of early (<=90-day) mortality after AF ablation not well characterized.
- Identifying causes needed to improve safety.

STUDY DESIGN + SETTING:
- Retrospective single-health-system cohort analysis.
- Mayo Clinic, 3 sites (Rochester, Phoenix, Jacksonville).
- Enrollment window: Jan 1, 2013 to Dec 1, 2021.
- Cause of death ascertained via comprehensive EHR chart review plus outside records when available.

POPULATION / SAMPLE / N:
- Total patients undergoing AF catheter ablation: n = 6723.

INTERVENTION / COMPARISON:
- No comparison arm; descriptive analysis of AF ablation outcomes.
- Outcome of interest: early mortality defined as death <=90 days post-ablation.

KEY RESULTS (exact numbers):
- 90-day all-cause mortality rate = 0.22% (n = 15).
- Of all 90-day deaths, 73.3% had NO direct relationship to the procedure.
- Most common cause of early death = sudden death, 20%.
- Other causes: peri-procedural stroke 13%; respiratory failure 13%; atrioesophageal fistula 13%; infection 7%; heart failure 7%; traumatic brain injury 7%.
- 90-day mortality directly due to AF ablation procedural complications = 0.06% (n = 4).

MAIN TAKEAWAY:
- AF ablation early mortality is low (90-day mortality 0.22%); procedure-attributable mortality very low (0.06%).
- Most early deaths (73.3%) not directly tied to a procedural complication.
- Sudden death is the leading cause of early death.
- Further study needed on causes/risk factors for sudden death in this population.
